metadata.title: Why Agents Forget — And the Memory Store That Fixes It
metadata.source: agents-that-remember/ — Code with Claude 2026 Workshop W3

beats.narration_text: Same agent. Same person. You tell it something in one session — your coding style, your stack, your preferences. Ask the same thing in the next session: nothing. This is Liam, in for Bear.

props.segment: Why Agents Forget — And the Memory Store That Fixes It
props.command: My agent forgets what I told it. Same agent, new session — blank slate every time. How do I give it a memory?

beats.narration_text: The question: if both sessions run the same agent, why does amnesia feel inevitable — and what layer actually enables recall?

props.sparkLine: Same agent. Two sessions. No memory.

beats.narration_text: Sessions are isolated by design. Each one starts with a fresh context window. The agent has no concept of 'last time.' That isolation is not a bug — it's the default. A memory store is what you attach to break it.

Here is the timeline. Session A: you tell the agent you prefer async/await over promises. No memory store — the agent hears it, responds, the session ends. Session B: you ask 'what's my coding style?' The agent has no idea. It was never written anywhere. Now attach a memory store. Session A writes a structured entry: 'User prefers async/await.' Session B opens, reads the store, finds the entry. 'You prefer async/await over promises.' Same agent. Different result. One lever changed.

The memory store handles persistence. The Dreaming Service goes one step further — it reads past transcripts, consolidates them into structured memories, and writes them back. The progression is isolation, then persistence, then self-improvement. Memory isn't built in. It's a separate lever you attach.

What does the memory store actually hold? Not prose — structured records. Each entry has four fields that matter. User ID: the store is scoped, so memories stay separated per user. Key: the retrieval handle — this is how the agent finds the memory later. Value: the content — the actual fact. And confidence: a decimal between zero and one representing how certain the agent was when it wrote this entry. Here's a real example: key equals prefers-async, value equals true, confidence 0.9. A second record appears alongside it: key equals timezone, value US/Eastern. The store is a set of typed facts. Nothing more. The structure is what makes retrieval reliable — loose prose would not index well.

props.sparkLine: The store is just structured facts about users.

beats.narration_text: The Dreaming Service is a loop, not a daemon. It runs between sessions — not during them. Here is the cycle. Session ends: the transcript is saved. Dreaming runs: it reads the transcript, identifies facts worth keeping, and writes structured memory records back to the store. The next session opens: it loads those records and injects them into the context prefix before the agent reads the first message. The critical property is where it runs: between sessions, not during. There is zero latency impact on your conversation. The agent does not dream while you wait — it dreams while you are away.

props.sparkLine: Dreaming runs between sessions, not during.

beats.narration_text: How does the agent actually recall a fact? Four steps, left to right. One: a user message arrives. Two: before the agent reads it, a semantic lookup fires — an embedding similarity search against the memory store, finding records whose keys and values match the incoming query. Three: the top three matching memories are retrieved. Four: they are injected into the context prefix — above the system prompt, before the first message. The agent never asks for them. By the time it reads your question, the memories are already there. Retrieval is not a feature the agent invokes — it is infrastructure that runs ahead of it.

props.sparkLine: Retrieval happens before the first token.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Sessions are isolated by design — each starts blank. A memory store breaks that: the agent writes entries in one session and reads them in the next. The Dreaming Service consolidates past transcripts into structured memory automatically. Memory is a lever you attach, not a property of the model.

artifactLines: Sessions are isolated by design — each starts with a blank context window.
A memory store adds persistence: the agent reads and writes across sessions.
The Dreaming Service goes further — it consolidates past transcripts into structured memory.
Memory is a lever you attach. It is not built into the agent by default.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt: 'Attach a memory store to my agent and show me what it writes after this session — then prove it recalls it in the next one.' What you're looking for is the write-read cycle. Watch what structured entry the agent produces — is it a key-value pair, a short paragraph, a tagged note? Then open a new session, ask a question the first session answered, and see if it finds the entry. The interesting moment is not that it recalls — it's HOW it organizes what it writes. That structure is what determines recall quality in session three, four, ten. Take this prompt, run it on your own agent, and watch the memory grow. Liam, in for Bear.

props.segment: Why Agents Forget — And the Memory Store That Fixes It
props.command: Attach a memory store to my agent and show me what it writes after this session — then prove it recalls it in the next one.
